skill.name: billing-automation
skill.description: Build automated billing systems for recurring payments, invoicing, subscription lifecycle, and dunning management. Use when implementing subscription billing, automating invoicing, or managing recurring payment systems.
skill.summary: Build automated billing systems for recurring payments, invoicing, subscription lifecycle, and dunni...
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static external command and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and billing terms such as amount, payment, and HTML table markup. Human review found no malicious intent or prompt injection, but the playbook includes payment processor calls and unescaped invoice HTML examples that require a marketplace warning.

medium_findings.title: Payment Processor Call in Example Code
medium_findings.description: The playbook demonstrates a Stripe charge call that would contact a payment processor and charge a saved customer if copied into a live environment. This matches the billing purpose and is not malicious, but it can cause financial harm without sandbox controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample directly calls a payment processor charge API, and the skill warns not to charge real customers in testing. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because billing automation legitimately needs payment integrations.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped Invoice HTML Template
medium_findings.description: The invoice HTML example formats customer name, address, and line item content directly into an HTML template. If copied without escaping, customer-controlled billing fields could create HTML injection or stored cross-site scripting in rendered invoices.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The template inserts customer and line item values into HTML without showing escaping or sanitization. The example omits surrounding application context, so the exact exploitability depends on the implementation.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences or inline file references. They do not execute commands and no shell invocation syntax was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched backticks delimit Markdown examples and filenames, not executable Ruby or shell expressions. The files are documentation and contain no runnable script entry point.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Billing Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts occur on billing words and HTML table content, not on MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic algorithms. No evidence of cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain invoice descriptions, amount fields, and HTML table markup. None of the cited lines implement or recommend a cryptographic algorithm.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Identifier False Positives
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts appear on generated billing identifiers such as subscription and invoice IDs. They are ordinary domain model fields and do not collect hostnames, usernames, environment details, or operating system data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited identifiers are billing object IDs, not host or system discovery commands. No evidence of reconnaissance behavior appears near those lines.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Billing URL
low_findings.description: The playbook includes a hardcoded example.com billing URL inside a dunning email template. It is a documentation placeholder, but implementers should replace it with a configured application URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is clearly an example domain and does not indicate exfiltration. It still represents a network-facing billing link that must be configured safely in real deployments.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Payment Network Call
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code calls Stripe to create a charge for a customer. This should only run with sandbox credentials and explicit safeguards.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code shows a direct payment processor API call with customer and amount arguments.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTML Formatting Without Escaping
dangerous_patterns.description: Invoice fields are inserted into HTML through string formatting. Production implementations should escape user-controlled values and render with a trusted template engine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The format call inserts customer fields into HTML, but the broader application context is not present.

content.value_statement: Recurring billing creates errors when subscriptions, invoices, retries, taxes, and usage charges are handled manually. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured patterns for building safer billing automation.
actual_capabilities: Design subscription states for trials, active accounts, past due accounts, pauses, and cancellations.
Create billing cycle processing flows that generate invoices and handle payment results.
Plan dunning retries, customer notifications, grace periods, and cancellation behavior.
Calculate prorated plan changes, seat changes, usage charges, and regional taxes.
Produce invoice structure examples for PDF and HTML generation.
Provide checklists for audit trails, tax compliance, notifications, and billing edge cases.
limitations: It provides documentation and example patterns, not a complete production billing engine.
Payment processor code must be adapted for sandbox testing before any live charge attempt.
Tax rates and compliance guidance are simplified examples and require local expert review.
Invoice HTML examples need escaping and template hardening before production use.

use_cases.title: Launch SaaS subscriptions
use_cases.description: Define plans, trials, renewals, invoices, and payment handling for a new subscription product.

use_cases.title: Improve failed payment recovery
use_cases.description: Create retry schedules, dunning emails, grace periods, and cancellation rules for unpaid invoices.

use_cases.title: Add usage billing
use_cases.description: Model metered events, tiered pricing, volume pricing, and billing period calculations.

prompt_templates.title: Map my billing flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to map a monthly subscription billing flow for my product. Include trial, renewal, invoice, payment success, failed payment, and cancellation states.

prompt_templates.title: Design dunning rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a failed payment recovery workflow with retry timing, email notices, grace periods, and account access changes.

prompt_templates.title: Calculate plan change proration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to specify proration behavior for upgrades, downgrades, added seats, removed seats, and annual-to-monthly plan changes.

prompt_templates.title: Review billing implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my billing design for lifecycle gaps, payment safety, tax handling, invoice states, usage billing, and audit trail needs.

output_examples.input: Create a billing lifecycle for a SaaS product with a 14 day trial.
output_examples.output: A state plan covering trial creation, trial end, activation, invoice generation, payment success, past due handling, retry attempts, pause options, and cancellation.

output_examples.input: Design dunning for failed card payments.
output: Retry schedule at three, seven, and fourteen days.
Customer notices for first failure, reminder, recovery, and final cancellation.
Grace period rules that balance revenue recovery with customer experience.

output_examples.output: A checklist covering line items, subtotal, tax, total, invoice status, delivery, PDF or HTML rendering, audit logging, and escaping of customer fields.

best_practices: Use sandbox payment credentials and test customers until every billing path is verified.
Keep an audit trail for invoices, payment attempts, retries, credits, cancellations, and tax calculations.
Validate tax, VAT, GST, and invoice requirements with qualified regional guidance before launch.
anti_patterns: Do not run charge examples against real customer payment methods during development.
Do not hardcode tax rates, billing URLs, plan IDs, or retry policies in production code.
Do not render invoice HTML with unescaped customer names, addresses, or line item text.

faq.question: Does this skill process payments directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and examples that must be connected to a payment processor by your application.

faq.question: Can it help with Stripe billing?
faq.answer: Yes. The playbook includes payment processor concepts and a Stripe-style charge example, but production code needs sandbox testing.

faq.question: Does it handle tax compliance automatically?
faq.answer: No. It explains tax calculation structure, but real tax rates and compliance requirements need qualified review.

faq.question: What billing models are covered?
faq.answer: It covers recurring subscriptions, trials, prorations, seat changes, usage billing, tiered pricing, volume pricing, and dunning.

faq.question: Is this ready for production use?
faq.answer: No. Treat it as a design and implementation guide. Harden payments, templates, tax logic, and audit logging before launch.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
